main_summary.summary: Bill Russell, interviewed in his home by Taylor Branch on May 13, 2013, for the Library of Congress and the National Museum of African American History and Culture. Russell traces his family's path from West Monroe, Louisiana, to Detroit, then Oakland, after his father Charles "Mr. Charlie" Russell left a foundry job over a refused raise and racial slur. He recounts his grandfather facing down the Ku Klux Klan with a shotgun loaded with birdshot, and the family's purchase of lumber to build a Black schoolhouse. Russell describes his mother Katie's death when he was twelve, his father's promise to raise the boys himself, and his middle name Felton after Southern University president Felton G. Clark. The bulk of the interview covers his basketball career: McClymonds High School, University of San Francisco under coach Phil Woolpert (1952-1956, including back-to-back NCAA championships and a 55-game win streak), the 1956 Olympic gold medal, and thirteen seasons with the Boston Celtics including eleven championships and his 1966 hire as the NBA's first Black head coach. Russell discusses Red Auerbach, Walter A. Brown, teammates Bob Cousy, Sam Jones, Tom Heinsohn, K.C. Jones, Frank Ramsey, John Havlicek, and Tom "Satch" Sanders. He addresses his trip to Jackson, Mississippi, in 1964 to support Charles Evers after Medgar Evers's assassination, the Cleveland summit supporting Muhammad Ali's draft refusal organized by Jim Brown, his pall-bearer role at Jackie Robinson's funeral, his autograph policy, his refusal to engage with the Red Sox until they joined his Mentoring USA board, and the Boston statue dedicated in 2013.
key_themes: Family migration from Jim Crow Louisiana to wartime Oakland
Basketball career arc: USF, Olympics, Boston Celtics
Race relations in Boston and integration of the NBA
Mentoring philosophy and Mentoring USA foundation
Solidarity with Muhammad Ali, Jackie Robinson, and the civil rights movement
main_summary.historical_significance: Russell provides primary-source testimony on multiple integration firsts in American sports: the Celtics drafting Chuck Cooper (first Black NBA draftee, 1950), drafting Sam Jones from a historically Black college in the first round, fielding the first all-Black starting five, and hiring Russell himself as the first Black head coach in any major American professional sport in 1966. He documents his family's experience of the Great Migration in personal detail (his father's confrontation with a foundry boss over a raise refused on racial grounds, the draft-board retaliation, the move to Detroit then Oakland's shipyards). The interview records his June 1964 trip to Jackson, Mississippi, to run integrated basketball clinics for Charles Evers a year after Medgar Evers's assassination, his participation in the 1967 Cleveland summit organized by Jim Brown supporting Muhammad Ali's stand on the Vietnam draft, his presence at the 1963 March on Washington, and his service as the only non-Brooklyn Dodger pall-bearer at Jackie Robinson's funeral. Russell's recollections of teammates and Red Auerbach provide on-the-record evidence of how a single franchise, under owner Walter A. Brown, moved against the racial conventions of mid-century professional sports.

chapters.title: The Laugh, and a Character Built on Love
chapters.summary: Russell traces his famous laugh to his mother's advice never to hold anything back, and roots his character in never doubting that both parents loved him, and in his father's lesson that other people's problems with him were their own little red wagon.

chapters.title: The Old Man Faces Down the Klan
chapters.summary: He tells how his grandfather, the old man, scattered a Klan mob with birdshot and later, finding no school for Black children, organized neighbors to buy lumber and build a schoolhouse over a white merchant's threats, then raised fifty-three dollars to hire a teacher.

chapters.title: Named for a College President
chapters.summary: Russell describes his family's leap from a grandfather with no schooling to a daughter at Harvard Law, his mother's reverence for education that led her to name him Felton after a university president, and her deathbed plea that his father send the boys to college.

chapters.title: A Widower Raises His Two Boys
chapters.summary: After his mother died in 1946 when he was twelve, his father refused to let relatives split the boys up, sold his business, and took a foundry job at a fraction of the pay so he could be home every night, and Russell recalls their cooking rotation and the father who once carried the whole family across a field.

chapters.title: A Hundred Thousand Dollars, and a Father's Pride
chapters.summary: When Russell heard Wilt Chamberlain had signed for a hundred thousand dollars he demanded a dollar more, then called his father to say he could stop working, only to hear his father refuse the money: he had given the factory his best years and would now give them the bad ones.

chapters.title: Leaving Louisiana in the Great Migration
chapters.summary: Russell recounts his father's place in the Great Migration, a boss who refused a raise and used a slur to his face, his father's fear that staying would end in killing, and the move to Detroit and Ford against a draft-board threat meant to keep him in the plant.

chapters.title: Oakland, Shipyards, and a Fruit Truck
chapters.summary: Finding Detroit too cold, his father moved the family to Oakland for shipyard work, and when the war ended bought a truck and built a business hauling fruit pickers, the labor that bought young Russell his school clothes.

chapters.title: Cut From the Team, Found by a Coach
chapters.summary: Raised by a mother who kept him away from white people, Russell hit cultural shock at the University of San Francisco, where he was cut from the junior varsity until a varsity coach, his old junior-high teacher, took him on and bought him a year at the boys' club where he taught himself to play.

chapters.title: A Coach Who Hated His Game
chapters.summary: Russell tells how his college coach saw his style as a waste of a scholarship, how four varsity players objected to playing with Black teammates, and how in his first varsity game he blocked five straight shots only to be told that was not how to play defense.

chapters.title: Bringing the Vertical Game
chapters.summary: He describes bringing a vertical game to a sport that forbade leaving your feet, a leap that could mark chalk thirteen feet up, and how, though his purist coach never approved, he and his teammates won fifty-five straight games and back-to-back national championships.

chapters.title: Learning the Game by Imagination
chapters.summary: Russell explains how he used his imagination to learn basketball, studying first-class players down to their footwork and head fakes and then trying the moves himself the next day, picturing what he would become before he arrived.

chapters.title: Finish Every Fight You Start
chapters.summary: He remembers his mother marching him through the projects to fight five boys one at a time, telling him it mattered not that he lost but that he stood up for himself, a toughness balanced by his philosophical father, whom he later thanked with a car that earned the old man his first speeding ticket.

chapters.title: 1956: Final Four, Olympics, and Gold
chapters.summary: Russell recalls the extraordinary year of 1956, a second straight national title over Iowa and an Olympic gold medal won by beating the Soviet Union by thirty-five, before he married and headed to Boston as a near amateur.

chapters.title: The Celtics' Quiet Firsts
chapters.summary: He credits owner Walter Brown and the Celtics with a run of firsts often overlooked: the first Black player drafted in Chuck Cooper, the first team to start five Black players, almost by accident, and the first to draft a player from a Negro college in the first round, Sam Jones.

chapters.title: Becoming the First Black Coach
chapters.summary: Russell describes how Red Auerbach, retiring, promised to hire no coach Russell did not approve, and how after their lists of names matched no one, Russell took the job himself in 1966 as a player-coach, refusing an assistant in the charged Boston atmosphere.

chapters.title: Red Auerbach's System
chapters.summary: He recounts learning to coach as captain when Auerbach was ejected, the late-night gin games where they talked philosophy, Auerbach's rule of taking players as they were and building a place for each in the system, and his willingness to get thrown out of games to fight for his team.

chapters.title: Sharman's Fists and Protecting Cousy
chapters.summary: Russell remembers Bill Sharman, the most thoughtful player he knew and a constant fighter, and the night Auerbach had Jim Loscutoff answer Syracuse's beatings of Bob Cousy by flattening their best player Dolph Schayes, until the fans waved cardboard hatchets.

chapters.title: Guarding Behind Cousy, and the MVP Snub
chapters.summary: He explains how he covered for Cousy's defense by steering shooters into the lane, turning Russell's blocks and Cousy's steals into fast breaks, and how even after winning the players' MVP overwhelmingly he was named only second-team all-league, the only man to do both.

chapters.title: Why Cousy Called Him Arnold
chapters.summary: Russell tells how Auerbach, true to his theory of building a team to beat the best, drafted a big man over hometown favorite Bob Cousy and was crucified by a Boston press that also threw antisemitic threats at him, all of which Auerbach met with their little red wagon.

chapters.title: The Scout Who Said They Couldn't Play
chapters.summary: He relishes the Oklahoma City tournament where a Wichita scout, having watched their one loss, told his team that USF could not play a lick, only to be buried by K.C. Jones's full-court press, a rout that led Auerbach's old coach to call him at midnight about the player to get for Boston.

chapters.title: Remembering Every Play
chapters.summary: Russell describes a memory that let him narrate a decades-old game his late wife found on tape before each play happened, a recall he built for scouting so his defense could be action rather than reaction, and which he has since tried to forget.

chapters.title: McClymonds: Frank Robinson and Johnny Mathis
chapters.summary: He recalls the talent that came out of McClymonds High and his American Legion coach, schoolmates Frank Robinson and Curt Flood, and his friend Johnny Mathis, a fellow high-jumper whose acapella song at a sports dinner left the room in stunned silence.

chapters.title: Sam Jones and the Poet Heinsohn
chapters.summary: Russell paints Sam Jones, who could have been an all-time great but never wanted the daily responsibility yet never missed the shot that decided a season, and Tom Heinsohn, seen as a bruiser but really an artist and poet whom Auerbach used as a whipping boy.

chapters.title: Ramsey, the Sixth Man, and Mutual Respect
chapters.summary: He describes Frank Ramsey, whom Auerbach turned into the proud sixth starter rather than a substitute, the mutual respect between two teammates from opposite worlds, and the night Ramsey answered Russell's knocked-out teeth by pulling out his own bridge.

chapters.title: The Celtic Way: Kindness as Strength
chapters.summary: Russell explains the Celtic way, every play built from five men each doing what they did well, and his belief that kindness is an act of strength, illustrated by John Havlicek's shooting and by Satch Sanders rattling Bill Bradley with a line Russell fed him at the foul line.

chapters.part: Race On the Court and in Boston
chapters.title: King Kong, and Why He Wouldn't Fight
chapters.summary: He offers his own reading of King Kong as a story of sacrifice, his high-school coach's warning that a fighting Black team would be branded thugs, and how he answered a Fort Wayne center who hit him not with his fists but with a spike learned in the shipyards.

chapters.part: Race On the Court and in Boston
chapters.title: The Last Team to Integrate
chapters.summary: Russell recalls a Boston where the Red Sox were the last team to integrate and the busing fights ran hot, and how after a 1969 championship a reporter asked Auerbach whether a better coach would have won more, a slight that helped settle his decision to retire.

chapters.part: Race On the Court and in Boston
chapters.title: The Books, and a Contract He Wouldn't Collect
chapters.summary: He tells how Walter Brown opened the Celtics' books to show they could not yet pay him what he was worth, and how Russell, given an eight-year no-cut contract he could have drawn without playing, refused to take money he had not earned, just as his father had.

chapters.title: Sport as Art and War
chapters.summary: Russell describes thinking philosophically about sport as a mixture of art and war that gathers people, anchored by the conviction that it is far more important to understand than to be understood, and that happiness follows from taking control through understanding.

chapters.title: The Best Player He Could Be
chapters.summary: He recounts being passed over for a regional player award the year he was a first-team All-American, and how he simply dismissed the snub, refusing to tie his self-esteem to what others said and fixing instead on being the best player he could be.

chapters.title: Looking Out for Each Other
chapters.summary: Russell argues the Celtics' fun was essential to winning because they looked out for one another, and remembers a bad night when K.C. Jones deliberately picked a fight to fire him up, taking a chance on his behalf because he could see Russell needed help.

chapters.title: George Mikan's Kindness
chapters.summary: Against memories of minor-league players who spat slurs at the boys outside the ballpark, Russell sets George Mikan, the era's biggest star, who treated him kindly as an unknown teenager and years later asked the favor of introducing Russell to his son.

chapters.title: No Such Thing as Other People's Kids
chapters.summary: He shares his rule of waiting for the second thought so as never to injure a child, and the Fort Worth speech where he answered a man who resented taxes for other people's kids: there are no other people's children, only the next generation of Americans.

chapters.title: Medical Clinics in Mississippi
chapters.summary: Wary of organizations that could dismiss him as a mouthpiece, Russell still answered Charlie Evers's call after Medgar's murder, going to Jackson in 1964 to run open basketball and medical clinics for any child who came, guarded discreetly like a man under the Secret Service.

chapters.title: Do Not Be Afraid
chapters.summary: Recalling that he went to Mississippi the summer three civil rights workers were murdered their first night, Russell passes on the lesson a young Colorado player told him she would carry for life: do not be afraid, because decisions made out of fear are usually bad.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

chapters.title: The March, and the Night King Died
chapters.summary: He recounts briefly knowing Malcolm X, his affection for the gold-medalist Cassius Clay, declining King's invitation to the March on Washington stage because he had not done the work, and playing a game the night King was killed to keep a crowd calm and off the streets.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: Standing With Muhammad Ali
chapters.summary: Russell calls Ali a great boxer, psychologist, and brave man, refuses a demand that he disavow him even at the cost of being mocked as Felton X, and explains joining Jim Brown's group of athletes to support Ali after his draft status was changed arbitrarily without a hearing.

chapters.title: The Beard, the White Shoes, and Being True
chapters.summary: Asked about his most controversial stands, Russell offers his refusal to shave his beard or trade his white shoes for the team's black ones, lessons in analyzing from his own perspective that he later pressed on Bill Walton and Yao Ming, telling the latter to embrace being a very tall Chinese man.

chapters.title: Never Made Peace With Boston
chapters.summary: He says flatly that his peace with Boston never came, that he kept control and let the media's misconceptions pass, and dismisses greatest-player debates as unanswerable before recalling a CBS production meeting where he told an announcer he knew more about basketball than anyone he would meet.

chapters.title: Calling the Game Before It Happened
chapters.summary: Russell lays out the matchup analysis by which he predicted the Lakers would beat the Sixers with a rookie Magic Johnson at center, every adjustment, every speed mismatch, every familiarity, and how the game then unfolded exactly as he had told the disbelieving announcer.

chapters.title: I Don't Sign Autographs
chapters.summary: He dates his no-autograph policy to a 1964 decision over signing basketballs, frames it as a matter of free will that not even God could override, notes the counterfeit Russell signatures teammates forged, and recalls offering kids a conversation instead of a name.

chapters.title: I Played for the Celtics, Not Boston
chapters.summary: Russell insists he played for the Celtics rather than Boston, jokes about Jack Kent Cooke's offer to make him Wilt's backup, and explains why he would have nothing to do with the Red Sox, whose owner had vowed never to field a Black player, until they showed a real program for change.

chapters.title: Mentoring, and the Red Sox Make Amends
chapters.summary: He describes his one-to-one Mentoring charity, open to any child and grown to millions of volunteers, his condition that a Red Sox owner join its board to make real policy, and how the team then embraced mentoring at Fenway until Russell finally threw out a first pitch.

chapters.title: The Statue and the Tombstone
chapters.summary: Russell recounts resisting a statue that sounded to him like a tombstone, being persuaded by Mayor Menino that it was for the city, and shaping it at City Hall Plaza with a Valley of Champions and his own sayings, tied to a legacy foundation funding mentoring and education over war.

chapters.title: A Good Second to His Father
chapters.summary: Asked about the Presidential Medal of Freedom from President Obama, Russell calls it a good strong second to the day his father told him at seventy-five that he was proud to be his son and that Russell was his hero.

chapters.title: Pallbearer for Jackie Robinson
chapters.summary: He describes the honor of being the only non-Dodger pallbearer at Jackie Robinson's funeral, called by Rachel Robinson because he had been Jackie's favorite athlete, and frames his own role as carrying the game from point B to C after Jackie took it from A to B, with the first Black coaching job mattering only once race no longer governed hiring.

chapters.title: Rivals, and Respecting Every Approach
chapters.summary: Russell says rivals like Wilt Chamberlain and Elgin Baylor made him better, recounts apologizing to Wilt for words said in jest, and extends the same respect to the many civil rights organizations and to Nelson Mandela, whom he honored for refusing to disavow violence and abandon his comrades.

chapters.title: No Regrets, and a Changed Game
chapters.summary: He voices no real regrets beyond wishing he could have done more to expose racism, and reflects on a changed NBA he will not compare across eras, noting that he effectively invented the blocked shot at eighteen and that the zone defense he once held in contempt is now integral.

chapters.title: What I Do, Not What I Am
chapters.summary: Russell closes with advice to approach what you do well professionally, the airport exchange where he insisted basketball was what he did and not what he was, the total team concept behind the championships, and his reasons for never returning to a West Monroe whose sheriff once humiliated his mother.
keywords: what I do not what I am

loc_video.caption: Bill Russell oral history interview conducted by Taylor Branch in Seattle, Washington, 2013 May 12
